critique.summary: The article describes the development and features of pperl, a new Perl 5 interpreter written in Rust with AI assistance, focusing on autoparallelization, JIT compilation, and native Rust module integration. It also details the author's work on home automation systems using Perl and CAN bus technology, and the use of AI in creating Perl tools and modules.
mainPoints: pperl is a Perl 5 interpreter implemented in Rust, designed for high compatibility with Perl 5.42 and enhanced performance through JIT and autoparallelization.
The interpreter uses Rust's Rayon library for automatic parallel execution and Cranelift for JIT compilation, achieving significant speedups over standard Perl 5.
The author has developed a home automation system (WHIP) using Perl, STM32 microcontrollers, and CAN bus technology, emphasizing reliability and long-term operation.
AI has been used extensively to write Perl code, develop tools like SNMP::MIB::Compiler and Grpc::FFI, and assist in the pperl project.
pperl includes features like Auto-FFI for calling C libraries without XS, bytecode caching for faster startup, and a daemon mode for efficient script execution.
critique.truthfulness: The article's technical claims about pperl's design, use of Rust, Rayon, Cranelift, and the described home automation system align with known technologies and practices. The performance benchmarks and feature descriptions are plausible and consistent with the capabilities of these tools. The historical references to other Perl interpreter projects and the challenges of Perl 5 compatibility are accurate. However, the article is primarily authored by the project creator and lacks independent third-party sources or external validation, which limits the ability to fully verify all claims. The AI contributions described are plausible given recent advances but are anecdotal without external corroboration.
considerations: The article does not provide external or independent evaluations of pperl's performance or compatibility beyond internal benchmarks.
There is limited discussion of potential limitations or challenges in adopting pperl for existing Perl codebases, especially large or complex ones.
The home automation system described is specific to the author's projects and may not represent general solutions or standards.
The role of AI in code generation is presented positively but lacks detailed evidence or comparison to other AI-assisted programming efforts.
Security implications of a new interpreter and its ecosystem are not deeply addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around a new Perl JIT project that aims to autoparallelize Perl code using AI, with users commenting on the project's features, usability issues with the presentation slides, and comparisons to existing Perl tools. Some comments highlight technical details and express excitement, while others note navigation problems and request source code access.

comments.author: chrisaycock
comments.text:
The project relies on Rayon [1] for scheduling parallel tasks and Cranelift [2] to JIT the hot loops.

There are plenty of other interesting features like auto-FFI, bytecode caching (similar to Python's .pyc files), and "daemonize" mode (similar to mod_perl or FastCGI).

[1] https://docs.rs/rayon/latest/rayon/

[2] https://cranelift.dev
comments.analysis: This comment provides accurate technical details about the project's dependencies and features, showing a good understanding of the implementation.

comments.author: petre
comments.text:
Where's the codebase?

I had to build a Perl implementation of the Chaskey mac algorithm. ChatGPT spat out a working Perl prototype based on a C file for Arduino. It quite slow with not very much to optimize, so I made it write it with XS. A hour later I have a working XS implementation that compiles and tests cleanly.

So the AutoFFI thing is super interesting. The .plc also.
comments.analysis: The comment raises a valid question about code availability and shares relevant personal experience with Perl and AI, adding credibility.

comments.author: joosters
comments.text: Awesome to see a perl JIT. I love perl, and it's exciting to see something that tries to offer good-enough compatibility to run most perl code.
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm and a positive outlook on the project’s compatibility goals, reflecting a genuine user perspective.

comments.author: 0xbadcafebee
comments.text:
WHOA. Talk about burying the lede... Look from the beginning of the slide show, he made a super cool geothermal project! Look at the size of this hole!! https://perl.petamem.com/gpw2026/perl-mit-ai-gpw2026.html#/1... His cad drawings are great too!

Basically he wanted home automation in Perl to control his geothermal/solar house, and ended up reimplementing Perl with AI. That's some yak shaving...
comments.analysis: Provides interesting background context linking the project to a personal automation goal, though somewhat anecdotal.

comments.author: rurban
comments.text:
The docs are better than the slides.

https://perl.petamem.com/docs/eng/petaperl/differences.html
comments.analysis: Helpful pointer to documentation, suggesting the slides may be insufficient, which aligns with other users’ navigation complaints.

comments.author: quantummagic
comments.text: I'm interested, but can't navigate the website.  The down-arrow in the lower-right is unclickable, maybe covered by some semi-transparent chrome of my browser, not sure.  And no idea why there need to be 4 directional arrows.
comments.analysis: Valid usability feedback about navigation issues, corroborated by other users, though speculative about cause.

comments.author: hintymad
comments.text: The slides got stuck at https://perl.petamem.com/gpw2026/perl-mit-ai-gpw2026.html#/6. The right arrow disappeared. The down arrow was flashing, but did not respond to any clicks. I tried different browsers on my mac. None worked.
comments.analysis: Confirms navigation problems across browsers, strengthening the credibility of reported UI issues.

comments.author: bheadmaster
comments.text:
The down arrow doesn't respond because of the overlay page number. Only when clicking a little bit left of the overlay, it will work.

I can't help but giggle at the fact that AI written project doesn't seem to get its home page right.
comments.analysis: Provides a plausible explanation for the UI issue but includes a sarcastic tone that diminishes constructive value.

comments.author: genpfault
comments.text:
Not to be confused with GNU parallel[1], written in Perl.

[1]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GNU_parallel
comments.analysis: Clarifies a potential confusion with another Perl-related parallel tool, adding helpful context.

comments.author: postepowanieadm
comments.text: I'm too scared to check how good llms are in writing perl.
comments.analysis: Expresses apprehension without substantive content or constructive input.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.analysis: Irrelevant question unrelated to the discussed project, adding no value to the conversation.

comments.author: bmn__
comments.text:
Homepage: https://perl.petamem.com

In case HN shows its user hostility again by cutting off the URI fragment, the intended deep-link was presentation slide #/4/1/1
comments.analysis: Provides useful navigation information for accessing the presentation, aiding other users.
